does: This paper introduces VERIRL, a reinforcement learning framework designed to improve large language model-based Verilog code generation. The work constructs Veribench-53K, a curated dataset of 53,000 high-quality Verilog problems derived from over 700,000 examples, annotated with structured prompts, complexity labels, and diverse testbenches. The core technical contributions address two critical challenges: a Trace-back based Rescore mechanism that improves reward signal quality by leveraging reasoning paths and iterative refinement to enable more reliable reward model training, and a sample-balanced weighting strategy that adaptively balances learning during RL fine-tuning based on reward-probability distributions to prevent catastrophic forgetting and overfitting. These components are integrated into an iterative RL pipeline that co-evolves the code generation model.
connects: This work builds on recent progress in LLM-based code generation while addressing the specific challenges of hardware description languages that have limited prior exploration. It extends reinforcement learning approaches previously applied to general programming tasks to the domain of Verilog, which presents unique difficulties including concurrency semantics, strict syntactic requirements, and expensive simulation feedback. The research responds to limitations in existing reward mechanisms for code generation by developing more robust signal processing through trace-based analysis. The approach combines insights from reward modeling in RL, curriculum learning through complexity labels, and modern fine-tuning techniques adapted for the hardware design domain where sparse and noisy rewards are particularly problematic.
enables: This framework enables improved automated generation of hardware description code, supporting faster hardware design iteration and potentially making HDL development more accessible to non-expert practitioners. The Trace-back Rescore mechanism and adaptive weighting strategy represent general RL techniques that could transfer to other domains with sparse, noisy, or expensive-to-obtain reward signals. The Veribench-53K dataset provides infrastructure for future research in hardware code generation and could facilitate development of specialized models for Verilog and related HDLs. By improving LLM performance on hardware design tasks, this work opens pathways toward AI-assisted chip design workflows and could accelerate research in areas like automated circuit optimization and hardware verification where code quality directly impacts system reliability.
